lessons.title: Introduction to Medical Word Parts
objectives: Explain why medical language is built from Greek and Latin word parts
Identify the three main components of a medical term: prefix, root, suffix
Demonstrate how combining forms connect root words to other word parts
Decode a simple two-part medical term using the word-part method

conceptBlocks.heading: Why Medical Language Uses Word Parts
conceptBlocks.body: Most medical terms are built from Greek and Latin roots. Because these languages are no longer in everyday use, their meanings remain stable across countries and centuries — a cardiologist in Brazil and one in Japan both understand 'cardio-' refers to the heart. Learning word parts is more efficient than memorizing thousands of isolated words: master roughly 200 word parts and you can decode tens of thousands of terms.

conceptBlocks.heading: The Three Building Blocks
conceptBlocks.body:
A medical term has up to three components:

• **Prefix** — placed before the root; modifies meaning (e.g., brady- = slow)
• **Root word** — the core meaning (e.g., cardi = heart)
• **Suffix** — placed after the root; often indicates a condition, procedure, or specialty (e.g., -itis = inflammation)

Not every term uses all three. Some terms are root + suffix; others add a prefix.

conceptBlocks.heading: Combining Forms
conceptBlocks.body:
When a root word must connect to another word part beginning with a consonant, a vowel — usually 'o' — is inserted. This is called a combining vowel, and the root + vowel is called a combining form.

Example: cardi/o + logy = cardiology (study of the heart)

The combining vowel is dropped when the suffix starts with a vowel: gastr + itis = gastritis (not gastr/o/itis).

conceptBlocks.heading: Decoding Strategy
conceptBlocks.body:
Work right to left when decoding:
1. Identify and define the suffix
2. Identify and define the prefix (if present)
3. Define the root or combining form
4. Combine the definitions into a working meaning

Example: pericarditis
→ suffix: -itis (inflammation)
→ prefix: peri- (around, surrounding)
→ root: card (heart)
→ Working meaning: inflammation surrounding the heart

lessons.teachingNotes: Introduce with a familiar word such as 'appendicitis' and walk students through the decode-right-to-left method before presenting new vocabulary. Emphasize that word parts are tools, not memorization exercises.

lessons.title: Common Prefixes in Healthcare
objectives: Recall at least 20 high-frequency medical prefixes and their meanings
Distinguish prefixes that indicate number, direction, time, size, or negation
Apply prefixes correctly to construct or decode clinical terms
Identify look-alike prefix pairs that are commonly confused (e.g., hyper- vs hypo-)

conceptBlocks.heading: Prefixes of Quantity and Size
conceptBlocks.body:
mono- / uni- = one (monocyte, unilateral)
bi- / di- = two (bilateral, diplopia)
tri- = three (trimester)
poly- / multi- = many (polyuria, multifocal)
micro- = small (microscope)
macro- / mega- = large (macrocyte, megacolon)
null- = none (nullipara)

conceptBlocks.heading: Prefixes of Position and Direction
conceptBlocks.body:
sub- = below, under (subcutaneous, sublingual)
supra- / super- = above (suprapubic, superior)
inter- = between (intercostal)
intra- = within (intravenous)
extra- = outside (extracellular)
peri- = around (pericardium)
trans- = across, through (transdermal)

conceptBlocks.heading: Prefixes of Negation and Opposition
conceptBlocks.body:
a- / an- = without, absence of (apnea, anemia)
non- = not (noncompliant)
anti- / contra- = against (antibiotic, contraindicated)
de- = removal, reversal (dehydration)

conceptBlocks.heading: Critical Look-Alike Pair: Hyper- vs Hypo-
conceptBlocks.body:
hyper- = excessive, above normal (hypertension = high blood pressure; hyperglycemia = high blood sugar)
hypo- = deficient, below normal (hypotension = low blood pressure; hypoglycemia = low blood sugar)

Confusing these prefixes in clinical documentation can be a safety error. Always verify which prefix a term carries before acting on it.

conceptBlocks.heading: Prefixes of Time
conceptBlocks.body:
pre- / ante- = before (prenatal, antepartum)
post- = after (postoperative)
neo- = new (neonatal)

lessons.teachingNotes: The hyper-/hypo- pair causes consistent student confusion and real-world clinical errors. Spend extra time here. Use mnemonics: hyPER = excessive (like a hyper child), hyPO = below (like a hippo low to the ground).

lessons.title: Common Suffixes and Their Meanings
objectives: Recall at least 20 high-frequency suffixes and their meanings
Distinguish suffixes that indicate conditions, procedures, specialists, or instruments
Recognize how the same root paired with different suffixes produces related but distinct terms
Use suffix knowledge to infer the category of an unfamiliar medical term

conceptBlocks.heading: Suffixes Indicating Conditions and Diseases
conceptBlocks.body:
-itis = inflammation (appendicitis, colitis)
-osis = abnormal condition, disease process (cyanosis, nephrosis)
-emia = blood condition (anemia, leukemia, septicemia)
-algia / -dynia = pain (neuralgia, pleurodynia)
-pathy = disease (cardiomyopathy, neuropathy)
-stenosis = narrowing (aortic stenosis)
-plegia = paralysis (paraplegia, hemiplegia)
-rrhea = flow, discharge (diarrhea, rhinorrhea)

conceptBlocks.heading: Suffixes Indicating Procedures
conceptBlocks.body:
-ectomy = surgical removal (appendectomy, cholecystectomy)
-otomy / -tomy = cutting into (laparotomy, tracheotomy)
-ostomy = creating a new opening (colostomy, tracheostomy)
-plasty = surgical repair (rhinoplasty, arthroplasty)
-scopy = visual examination (colonoscopy, bronchoscopy)
-graphy = process of recording (radiography, echocardiography)
-centesis = surgical puncture to remove fluid (amniocentesis, thoracentesis)

conceptBlocks.heading: Suffixes Indicating Specialists and Sciences
conceptBlocks.body:
-ology = study of; -ologist = specialist in the study of
cardiology / cardiologist
neurology / neurologist
oncology / oncologist

-iatry = medical specialty; -iatrist = specialist
psychiatry / psychiatrist
podiatry / podiatrist

conceptBlocks.heading: Suffixes Indicating Physical Characteristics
conceptBlocks.body:
-megaly = enlargement (hepatomegaly, cardiomegaly)
-ptosis = drooping, prolapse (blepharoptosis, nephroptosis)
-rrhage / -rrhagia = bursting forth, excessive flow (hemorrhage, menorrhagia)
-trophy = development, nourishment (atrophy, hypertrophy)
-penia = deficiency (thrombocytopenia, neutropenia)

lessons.teachingNotes: -ectomy vs -ostomy vs -otomy is a frequent test point. Emphasize: ectomy = out (removed), ostomy = new opening (bag), otomy = cut in (temporary). Use appendix-based examples: appendectomy = remove it; tracheotomy = cut in for airway.

lessons.title: Root Words by Body System
objectives: Recall the most common root words for each major body system
Match a root word to its body system of origin
Combine root words with prefixes and suffixes to form accurate medical terms
Recognize root words in context when reading clinical documentation

conceptBlocks.heading: Cardiovascular and Hematological Roots
conceptBlocks.body:
cardi/o = heart (cardiomegaly, cardiology)
angi/o / vas/o = vessel (angioplasty, vasoconstriction)
aort/o = aorta (aortic)
phleb/o / ven/o = vein (phlebitis, venous)
arteri/o = artery (arteriosclerosis)
hem/o / hemat/o = blood (hemoglobin, hematology)
thromb/o = clot (thrombosis)
leuk/o = white (leukocyte)

conceptBlocks.heading: Respiratory Roots
conceptBlocks.body:
pneum/o / pulmon/o = lung (pneumonia, pulmonary)
bronch/o = bronchus (bronchitis, bronchoscopy)
rhin/o = nose (rhinorrhea, rhinitis)
phar/yngo = pharynx (pharyngitis)
laryngo = larynx (laryngoscopy)
tracheo = trachea (tracheostomy)
pleur/o = pleura (pleuritis)

conceptBlocks.heading: Gastrointestinal Roots
conceptBlocks.body:
gastr/o = stomach (gastritis, gastroscopy)
enter/o = small intestine (enteritis)
col/o / colon/o = large intestine (colitis, colonoscopy)
hepar / hepat/o = liver (hepatitis, hepatomegaly)
cholecyst/o = gallbladder (cholecystectomy)
pancreat/o = pancreas (pancreatitis)

conceptBlocks.heading: Urinary, Neurological, and Musculoskeletal Roots
conceptBlocks.body:
nephr/o / ren/o = kidney (nephritis, renal)
ureter/o = ureter | cyst/o = bladder (cystitis)
neur/o = nerve (neurology, neuropathy)
encephal/o = brain (encephalitis)
myel/o = spinal cord / bone marrow (myelopathy)
oste/o = bone (osteoporosis)
myo / muscul/o = muscle (myopathy, muscular)
arth/r/o = joint (arthritis, arthroplasty)
derm/o / dermat/o = skin (dermatitis, dermatology)

lessons.teachingNotes: Organize root words by system so students build a mental map. Encourage students to create a personal reference card grouped by system. Emphasize that cyt/o (cell) and hist/o (tissue) are foundational roots that cross all systems.

questions.stem: A nursing student encounters the term 'bradycardia' in a patient chart. Using word-part analysis, what does this term mean?
questions.rationale: Brady- means slow, and cardi- refers to the heart. Bradycardia = slow heart rate. Tachy- means fast (tachycardia), while a- means absence (asystole). Arrhythmia refers to irregular rhythm.

questions.stem: Which suffix indicates the surgical removal of an organ or structure?
questions.rationale: -Ectomy means surgical removal (e.g., appendectomy = removal of the appendix). -Ostomy means creation of a new artificial opening. -Otomy means cutting into without full removal. -Plasty means surgical repair or reconstruction.

questions.stem: A patient is described as having 'hyperglycemia.' What does this term indicate?

options.C: Absence of glucose in the urine
options.D: Normal blood glucose with irregular fluctuations

questions.rationale: Hyper- means excessive or above normal; -glyc- refers to sugar/glucose; -emia means a blood condition. Hyperglycemia = high blood glucose. Hypoglycemia (hypo- = below normal) would be low blood glucose.

questions.stem: The combining vowel 'o' is inserted into a medical term primarily to:

options.A: Change the meaning of the root word
options.B: Indicate that a procedure is involved
options.C: Make the term easier to pronounce when joining word parts
options.D: Signal that the suffix is related to a disease condition

questions.rationale: The combining vowel (usually 'o') is added between a root word and another word part beginning with a consonant to ease pronunciation. It does not change the meaning of the root. It is dropped when the suffix begins with a vowel.

questions.stem: Which of the following terms means 'inflammation of the liver'?
questions.rationale: Hepat/o = liver; -itis = inflammation. Hepatitis = inflammation of the liver. Hepatomegaly = liver enlargement (-megaly). Hepatectomy = surgical removal of part of the liver (-ectomy). Hepatology = the study of the liver (-logy).

questions.stem: A prefix that means 'below' or 'under' is:
questions.rationale: Sub- means below or under (e.g., subcutaneous = under the skin). Supra- means above. Hyper- means above normal or excessive. Trans- means across or through.

questions.stem: The suffix '-penia' found in the term 'thrombocytopenia' means:
questions.rationale: -Penia means deficiency or decrease. Thrombocytopenia = deficiency of thrombocytes (platelets). -Osis means abnormal condition or disease process. -Rrhage/-rrhagia means bursting forth or excessive bleeding.

questions.stem: Which term describes a procedure in which a new artificial opening is surgically created?
questions.rationale: -Ostomy = creation of a new opening. A colostomy creates an opening from the colon through the abdominal wall. Colectomy = removal of the colon (-ectomy). Colonoscopy = visual examination of the colon (-scopy). Colotomy = cutting into the colon (-otomy).

questions.stem: The prefix 'peri-' in the term 'pericardium' means:
questions.rationale: Peri- means around or surrounding. The pericardium is the membrane surrounding the heart. Intra- means within or inside. Sub- means below. Inter- means between.

questions.stem: A student reads the term 'dyspnea' in a patient's chart. Using word-part analysis, dyspnea most accurately means:

options.D: Excessive breathing leading to low CO2

questions.rationale: Dys- = painful, difficult, or abnormal; -pnea = breathing. Dyspnea = difficult or painful breathing. Apnea (a- = without) = absence of breathing. Tachypnea = rapid breathing. Hyperventilation = excessive breathing with CO2 loss.

questions.stem: Which root word refers specifically to the kidney?
questions.rationale: Nephr/o (and ren/o) refer to the kidney. Hepat/o refers to the liver. Cyst/o refers to the bladder (or a cyst/sac). Enter/o refers to the small intestine.

questions.stem: The term 'hemiplegia' is correctly decoded as:

options.A: Partial weakness on both sides of the body
options.B: Complete paralysis of all four limbs
options.C: Paralysis of one side of the body
options.D: Paralysis of the lower body only

questions.rationale: Hemi- = half (one side); -plegia = paralysis. Hemiplegia = paralysis of one side of the body. Paraplegia (para- = beside/both) = lower body paralysis. Quadriplegia = all four limbs. Paresis refers to weakness, not complete paralysis.

questions.stem: A procedure in which a surgeon cuts into the trachea to establish an airway is called a:
questions.rationale: Tracheo = trachea; -otomy = cutting into. A tracheotomy is an incision into the trachea. A tracheostomy creates a permanent or semi-permanent opening with an external stoma. Tracheectomy would mean removal. Tracheoplasty = surgical repair.

questions.stem: The suffix '-ology' means:
questions.rationale: -Ology means the study of a subject. A cardiologist is the specialist (-ologist); cardiology is the study (-ology). -Iatry refers to a medical treatment specialty. -Itis refers to inflammation (a condition).

questions.stem: Which prefix means 'within' or 'inside,' as in a medication given directly into a vein?
questions.rationale: Intra- means within or inside (intravenous = within the vein). Extra- means outside. Inter- means between structures. Trans- means across or through.

questions.stem: The medical term for an abnormally low white blood cell count is:
questions.rationale: Leuk/o = white; cyt/o = cell; -penia = deficiency. Leukopenia = deficiency of white blood cells. Leukocytosis (-osis = abnormal increase) = elevated white cell count. Leukopoiesis = production of white cells. Leukorrhea = white discharge.

questions.stem: A patient's chart states she is 'nulliparous.' Using word-part analysis, this means the patient:

options.A: Has given birth to one child
options.B: Has given birth to multiple children
options.D: Is currently pregnant for the first time

questions.rationale: Nulli- = none; -para = to bear (give birth). Nulliparous = never having given birth. Primipara = first birth (primi- = first). Multipara = multiple births. Primigravida = pregnant for the first time (gravida = pregnant).

questions.stem: When decoding a medical term, what is the correct order of steps?

options.A: Define the prefix, then the root, then the suffix
options.B: Define the suffix first, then the prefix, then the root
options.C: Define the root first, then work outward to prefix and suffix
options.D: Read the entire term and match it to a memorized definition

questions.rationale: The standard medical terminology decoding method is: (1) define the suffix, (2) define the prefix if present, (3) define the root/combining form, then combine meanings. Starting with the suffix gives the category of term (condition, procedure, specialist, etc.) before refining with the root.

questions.stem: Which of the following terms contains a suffix indicating a disease condition or abnormal state?
questions.rationale: Cyanosis contains the suffix -osis, which means an abnormal condition or disease process (cyan/o = blue; cyanosis = bluish discoloration). Appendectomy (-ectomy = removal) is a procedure. Dermatology (-logy = study of) is a specialty. Arthroscopy (-scopy = visual examination) is a procedure.

questions.stem: The prefix 'anti-' in the term 'antibiotic' means:
questions.rationale: Anti- means against or opposing. An antibiotic works against bacteria. Pro- or 'for' would indicate promotion. Intra- means within. Pre- / ante- means before.

questions.stem: A physician orders an echocardiography for a patient. The suffix '-graphy' in this term refers to:

options.B: Visual examination of the heart using a scope
options.C: The process of recording an image of the heart
options.D: Removal of part of the heart wall

questions.rationale: -Graphy refers to the process of recording or producing an image. Echocardiography uses sound waves to record images of the heart. -Plasty = surgical repair. -Scopy = visual examination via scope. -Ectomy = removal.

questions.stem: The prefix 'poly-' in the term 'polyuria' means:
questions.rationale: Poly- means many or excessive. Polyuria = excessive urination (ur = urine; -ia = condition). An-, a- = absent. Dys- = painful or difficult. Brady- = slow.

questions.stem: Which suffix indicates enlargement of a structure?
questions.rationale: -Megaly means enlargement. Hepatomegaly = enlargement of the liver; cardiomegaly = enlarged heart. -Ptosis = drooping or prolapse. -Trophy = development or nourishment (atrophy = wasting, hypertrophy = overgrowth). -Rrhea = discharge or flow.

questions.stem: What is the difference between the terms 'nephritis' and 'nephrosis'?

options.A: Nephritis is a procedure; nephrosis is a study
options.B: Nephritis indicates inflammation; nephrosis indicates an abnormal disease condition
options.C: Nephritis means kidney removal; nephrosis means kidney drainage
options.D: Nephritis affects the left kidney only; nephrosis affects both kidneys

questions.rationale: Both terms share the root nephr/o (kidney). -Itis = inflammation (nephritis = kidney inflammation). -Osis = abnormal condition or disease process (nephrosis = degenerative kidney disease without inflammation as the primary feature). Understanding suffixes distinguishes between different categories of conditions.

questions.stem: A patient's chart notes a diagnosis of 'arthralgia.' This term means:
questions.rationale: Arthr/o = joint; -algia = pain. Arthralgia = pain in a joint. Arthritis (arth/o + -itis) = inflammation of a joint. Arthrodesis = surgical fusion. Arthropathy = disease of a joint (-pathy).

questions.stem: The prefix 'tachy-' is correctly used in which of the following terms?
questions.rationale: Tachy- means rapid or fast. Tachypnea = rapid breathing (-pnea = breathing). Tachycardia = rapid heart rate, not slow. Brady- means slow. Apnea = absence of breathing (a- = without).

questions.stem: A nurse sees the term 'subcutaneous' in a medication order. This route of administration means:
questions.rationale: Sub- = under, below; cutane/o = skin; -ous = pertaining to. Subcutaneous = under the skin. Intravenous = into a vein (intra- = within; ven/o = vein). Intramuscular = into muscle. Transmucosal = through mucous membranes.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a medical term that uses a combining vowel?
questions.rationale: Gastroenterology uses the combining form gastr/o (with combining vowel 'o') before another consonant-starting root (enter/o = intestine). Gastritis drops the combining vowel because -itis starts with a vowel. Gastrology = gastr + -ology (vowel dropped). Appendicitis = appendic + -itis (vowel dropped).

questions.stem: Which word part in 'osteosarcoma' refers to bone?
questions.rationale: Oste/o = bone. Osteo- is the combining form of the root 'oste' meaning bone. Sarc/o = flesh or connective tissue. -Oma = tumor. -Sarcoma = malignant tumor of connective tissue. Osteosarcoma = malignant tumor arising from bone.

questions.stem: The suffix '-centesis' in the term 'thoracentesis' means:
questions.rationale: -Centesis = surgical puncture to remove fluid. Thoracentesis = puncture of the thoracic cavity (pleural space) to drain fluid. -Scopy = visual examination. -Graphy = recording/imaging. -Ostomy = creating a new opening.

questions.stem: The prefix 'neo-' in the term 'neonatal' means:
questions.rationale: Neo- = new or recent. Neonatal = pertaining to the newborn (new birth) period. Post- = after. Pre- / ante- = before. Poly- / multi- = many.

questions.stem: Which term means 'abnormal condition of bluish skin discoloration'?
questions.rationale: Cyan/o = blue; -osis = abnormal condition. Cyanosis = an abnormal condition of bluish skin discoloration due to poor oxygenation. -Derma = skin (cyanoderma would be 'blue skin'). -Pathy = disease. -Tomy = incision.

questions.stem: A patient is described as 'postoperative.' The prefix 'post-' indicates:
questions.rationale: Post- means after. Postoperative refers to the period after a surgical operation. Pre- / ante- means before (preoperative = before surgery). Intra- means during or within (intraoperative).

questions.stem: The root word 'hem/o' or 'hemat/o' refers to:
questions.rationale: Hem/o and hemat/o both refer to blood. Hemoglobin = blood protein carrying oxygen. Hematology = study of blood disorders. Cardi/o = heart. Hepat/o = liver. Pneum/o or pulmon/o = lung.

questions.stem: Which suffix means 'pertaining to' and is commonly added to root words to create adjectives?
questions.rationale: The suffixes -ous, -al, and -ic all mean 'pertaining to' or 'relating to,' forming adjectives from root words (e.g., cardiac = pertaining to the heart; renal = pertaining to the kidney; cutaneous = pertaining to the skin). -Emia = blood condition. -Osis = abnormal condition. -Ectomy = surgical removal.

questions.stem: Which of the following terms contains the root for 'nerve'?
questions.rationale: Neur/o = nerve. Neuralgia = nerve pain (-algia = pain). Nephr/o = kidney (nephrology). Dermat/o = skin (dermatitis). My/o = muscle; cardi/o = heart (myocardium = heart muscle).

questions.stem: A nurse reads 'bilateral' in a patient's care plan. Using word-part analysis, bilateral means:

options.A: Pertaining to one side of the body
options.B: Pertaining to both sides of the body
options.D: Pertaining to the area between two structures

questions.rationale: Bi- = two; later/o = side; -al = pertaining to. Bilateral = pertaining to both (two) sides. Unilateral (uni- = one) = one side. Tri- = three. Inter- = between.

questions.stem: The suffix '-rrhage' or '-rrhagia' is correctly defined as:
questions.rationale: -Rrhage and -rrhagia mean bursting forth or excessive flow/bleeding. Hemorrhage = excessive bleeding (hem/o = blood). -Rrhea = flow or discharge (less severe). -Rrhaphy = surgical suturing. -Stenosis = narrowing.

questions.stem: Which term correctly describes the study of the skin and its disorders?
questions.rationale: Dermat/o = skin; -logy = study of. Dermatology = the study of the skin. Dermatitis (-itis) = inflammation of the skin. Dermatomy = not a standard term. Dermatosis (-osis) = an abnormal skin condition.

questions.stem: A nurse receives an order to administer a medication 'sublingually.' This means the medication should be given:

options.A: Under the skin with a needle
options.C: Into the stomach via a tube
options.D: Through the outer layer of the skin

questions.rationale: Sub- = under; lingu/o = tongue; -al = pertaining to. Sublingual = under the tongue. Subcutaneous = under the skin. Nasogastric (via tube to stomach) uses a different root. Transdermal = through the skin.

questions.stem: Which prefix means 'against' and is commonly used in drug names?
questions.rationale: Anti- means against. Anticoagulants work against coagulation; antiemetics work against nausea/vomiting. Pro- means before or in favor of. Auto- means self. Iso- means equal or same.

questions.stem: The root word 'thromb/o' refers to:
questions.rationale: Thromb/o refers specifically to a blood clot or thrombus. Thrombosis = formation of a blood clot within a vessel. Angi/o / vas/o = vessel. Thrombopoiesis refers to platelet production but the root 'thromb/o' itself means clot. -Itis = inflammation.

questions.stem: The term 'dysuria' is best defined as:
questions.rationale: Dys- = painful, difficult; ur/o = urine; -ia = condition. Dysuria = painful or difficult urination. Anuria (an- = without) = absence of urine. Nocturia = nighttime urination. Hematuria = blood in the urine (hemat/o = blood).

questions.stem: Which suffix means 'surgical suturing or repair of a wound'?
questions.rationale: -Rrhaphy means surgical suturing or repair by stitching. Herniorrhaphy = surgical repair of a hernia. -Centesis = surgical puncture to remove fluid. -Plasty = surgical reshaping or reconstruction. -Ectomy = surgical removal.

questions.stem: A nursing student reads the word 'atrophy' in a patient's chart. This term is best defined as:

options.A: Excessive growth or enlargement of a tissue
options.B: Wasting or decrease in the size of a tissue or organ

questions.rationale: A- = without; -trophy = development or nourishment. Atrophy = wasting away or decrease in size due to lack of use or nourishment. Hypertrophy (hyper- = excessive) = enlargement. Neoplasia = new abnormal growth. -Ectomy = surgical removal.

questions.stem: The root word 'my/o' or 'muscul/o' refers to:
questions.rationale: My/o and muscul/o both refer to muscle. Myocardium = heart muscle (cardi/o = heart). Myopathy = muscle disease. Oste/o = bone. Neur/o = nerve. Muc/o = mucus; muco/sa refers to mucous membranes.

questions.stem: Which of the following prefixes means 'across' or 'through'?
questions.rationale: Trans- means across or through. Transdermal = through the skin. Transfusion = passing (blood) across/through. Peri- = around. Inter- = between. Infra- = below or beneath.

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly distinguishes between 'microscopic' and 'macroscopic'?

options.A: Microscopic = visible to the naked eye; macroscopic = requires magnification
options.B: Microscopic = requires magnification to see; macroscopic = visible to the naked eye
options.C: Both terms describe the same level of visibility
options.D: Microscopic refers to tissue samples; macroscopic refers to whole organs only

questions.rationale: Micro- = small; macro- = large. Microscopic = so small it requires a microscope to see. Macroscopic = large enough to see without magnification (with the naked eye). These prefixes are fundamental in pathology and anatomy.
